insights.recommendation: Build a $1,000 starter emergency fund in a high-yield savings account before increasing investing.
insights.principle: Baby Step 1: a small emergency fund reduces the need to rely on credit when unexpected expenses hit.

sources.title: CFPB – Building emergency savings
sources.preview: Even small amounts saved regularly can help you cover unexpected costs.

sources.title: FinSight KB – Baby Steps
sources.preview: Emergency fund before aggressive debt payoff or investing.

insights.recommendation: Track spending for 2 weeks to close the gap between what you think you spend and what you actually spend.
insights.principle: The perception vs. reality gap is one of the biggest obstacles to student financial progress.

sources.title: FinSight KB – Behavioral finance
sources.preview: What people think they spend vs. what they actually spend.

insights.recommendation: Once you have a small emergency fund and consistent savings, consider a low-cost index fund like VOO or a target-date fund.
insights.principle: Bogle-style indexing: diversified, low-cost funds are appropriate for beginners.

sources.title: SEC investor.gov – Getting started
sources.preview: Basics of investing for beginners, including index funds.

action_plan.title: Open a high-yield savings account
action_plan.description: Set up automatic transfers of $50–100 per paycheck into a dedicated emergency fund.

action_plan.title: Track every dollar for 2 weeks
action_plan.description: Use a simple app or spreadsheet to log all spending and compare to your stated budget.

action_plan.title: Reassess readiness and next steps
action_plan.description: Review your savings rate and emergency fund; if both are on track, consider opening a brokerage and buying one index fund.

narrative: You're in a solid place to start building habits that will set you up long-term. Your spending gap and lack of an emergency fund are the two things to tackle first — both are very common and fixable. Once you have a small safety net and a clearer picture of where your money goes, you'll be in a much better position to start investing with confidence.
disclaimer: FinSight AI provides educational insights and planning support — not personalized financial advice. For major financial decisions, consult a certified financial planner.
